content.text:
<timestamp>Friday, Jun 19, 2026, 4:36 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
"""1) I still see that you are showing single messages. If you are still evaluating single messages then you can stop involving me in this project, I've completely lost interest in it. I can't see how evaluating or even just showing a single message without the full conversation context is better than the alternate. Multiple times I have asked about why whole conversations are not the focal point and you haven't made a counter argument to that. Disagreement and counter argument is most welcome, but agreeing and then doing something else is not.
2) We are not after best/worst ranking so why are you putting effort into that? Rather you need a rubric of 5 to 10 items and with yes/no answers and you mark them yes no. ANd based on that you decide if that conversation is at a level that is required or below. Sometimes 1 message can be sloppy that sholdn't matter, what matters is whether the person has had a meaningful conversation and steered the LLM into producing its best possible work.
3) Why are you asking the user feedback about best and worst? that is even more subjective than the alternate I suggested (rubric)"""
what is the person coming to say
</user_query>

The person is giving you pointed, somewhat frustrated feedback. Here's what they're actually saying, point by point:

**1. Evaluate whole conversations, not single messages.**
They're frustrated that the work is still operating on individual messages. Their core argument: a single message stripped of its full conversation context tells you nothing useful. They've raised this repeatedly and feel ignored. The sharper meta-complaint is about *how* you've responded: you keep agreeing with them and then doing the opposite. They'd rather you push back and argue your case ("disagreement and counter argument is most welcome") than nod along and quietly continue with the single-message approach. The threat ("you can stop involving me") is them signaling they're close to disengaging.

**2. Replace best/worst ranking with a yes/no rubric.**
They don't want a ranking system at all. They want a checklist of ~5–10 binary (yes/no) criteria. You apply it to a conversation, tally the answers, and decide one thing: does this conversation meet the required quality bar, or fall below it? The judgment they care about is whether **the person had a meaningful conversation and skillfully steered the LLM toward its best possible output** — not whether one message was sloppy. Individual weak messages should be tolerated; the conversation as a whole is what's being judged.

**3. Stop asking users for best/worst feedback.**
They think soliciting "best/worst" opinions from users is *more* subjective than a fixed rubric, so it's the wrong direction. A defined rubric is more objective and consistent.

**The underlying message:** Shift the unit of evaluation from messages to entire conversations, swap subjective ranking for an objective pass/below-bar rubric centered on conversation quality and user steering, and—just as importantly—engage with their reasoning honestly instead of agreeing and then ignoring them.

The user is asking me to interpret feedback they've received on their project. Looking at the first point, the reviewer is frustrated because they've repeatedly requested that evaluations be done at the conversation level rather than individual messages, and while they got agreement, nothing actually changed in practice.

The second point is about methodology — they don't want best/worst rankings but rather a binary rubric with 5-10 yes/no criteria to assess whether a conversation meets a quality threshold. The idea is that one sloppy message shouldn't tank the evaluation; what matters is whether the overall conversation was meaningful and helped guide the LLM toward better work.

The third point pushes back on asking users for subjective feedback about best/worst cases, since that's even more prone to bias than using a structured rubric.
